model_summary: Initial gVAR-ready observation model targets hyperarousal with four criterions (startle intensity, momentary on-edge tension, cue-linked physiological reactivity, and distress about symptoms) and six modifiable predictors spanning BIO and PSYCHO mechanisms (sleep quality, caffeine intake, paced breathing, mindfulness/grounding use, cognitive reappraisal, and gentle trigger approach). Sampling uses 5 EMA prompts/day (since-last-prompt or momentary) plus a morning diary for sleep across 21 days. Measures favor 1–9 Likert or low-burden counts to maximize within-person variance and minimize redundancy, with careful attention to potential collinearity (e.g., mindfulness and breathing) and nonlinear exposure effects (gentle trigger approach).
variable_selection_notes: Chosen criterions preserve the complaint’s resolution: (1) exaggerated startle (C01), (2) persistent on-edge tension (C02), (3) conditioned cue-reactivity (C03), and (4) distress about symptoms (C05). C04 (hypervigilance) overlaps with C02/C01 and was excluded to reduce collinearity; C06 (expectation–reality mismatch) is slower-moving and better captured via predictors (reappraisal) and outcome distress, so excluded to maintain estimability. Predictors emphasize high-signal, tractable levers supported by rankings and literature: sleep quality, caffeine modulation, autonomic downregulation (paced breathing), mindfulness/grounding, cognitive reappraisal, and graded trigger approach/exposure. Social/biomedical and passive-sensor options (e.g., HRV, broad social support) were deprioritized in this first pass to keep burden low and reduce construct redundancy. Uncertainty: gentle trigger approach can acutely increase arousal before longer-term reduction; marked as nonlinear with multi-lag. Within-person variance is expected to be adequate with 5/day sampling and since-last-prompt framing.

momentary_ema.notes: ~3–10 min jitter around anchors; reminders allowed once per prompt. Keep per-prompt item set short (~8–10 items).

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary asks about last night’s sleep before day EMA begins.

passive_streams.notes: Optional future add-ons: HRV (nightly), ambient noise events, step counts; start with EMA-only to reduce complexity.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: With 5 EMA prompts/day over 21 days, expect ~105 momentary observations/variable (pre-missingness) plus 21 sleep diaries. This supports person-specific gVAR with 10 variables (4 criteria, 6 predictors).
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use 1–9 Likert anchors, since-last-prompt recall, and time-block jitter to increase within-person variability and reduce recall bias. Include brief counts for skills use (log1p suggested).
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Highest risk is evening fatigue and weekend irregularity. Use 1 reminder/prompt, streak feedback, and <60s completion time. Monitor compliance; consider micro-incentives if drop-off >20%.

criteria_variables.label: Exaggerated startle intensity to sudden cues

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how strong were any startle/flinch reactions to sudden sounds or unexpected approaches?
measurement.notes: Optional event flag can be added later if needed; intensity captures both occurrence and magnitude.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how tense or on edge do you feel?
measurement.notes: Core hyperarousal criterion with strong momentary variance.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how strong was your body’s reaction when reminded of the past incident or similar cues? (If not reminded, choose 1)
measurement.notes: Combines reminder occurrence and intensity to avoid branching and reduce burden.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how much do you dislike or feel bothered by being jumpy/on edge?
measurement.notes: Captures emotional burden and motivation to change.

predictor_variables.label: Sleep quality last night
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Restorative sleep downregulates baseline arousal and improves emotion regulation, reducing startle and tension the following day.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Regular sleep/wake window, wind-down routine, light/dark management, bedroom noise reduction.

measurement.item_or_signal: Overall, how restorative was your sleep last night?
measurement.notes: Pairs with optional items later (sleep duration, awakenings) if needed.

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep lowers baseline hyperarousal and reduces startle reactivity across the day.
Poor sleep increases subjective tension; high-quality sleep decreases on-edge feelings next day.
Rested state improves inhibitory control and reduces cue-reactivity.
Restorative sleep buffers negative affect and symptom distress.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Non-pharmacologic sleep hygiene focus only; no dosing guidance.

feasibility.notes: Single-item morning diary is low burden.

predictor_variables.label: Caffeine intake since last prompt
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Caffeine increases sympathetic arousal and can heighten startle/tension; reducing intake may lower hyperarousal.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Cap intake earlier in day; swap to lower-caffeine or decaf; avoid energy drinks.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much caffeine did you consume (coffee, tea, soda, energy drinks)?
measurement.notes: Simple ordinal captures dose without complex mg estimation.

expected_effects.rationale: Acute stimulant effects increase startle probability/intensity.
Increases subjective jitteriness and tension.
Elevated arousal can potentiate cue-reactivity.
Jitteriness can raise frustration/distress about symptoms.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Avoids medical advice; reminds to consider personal sensitivity and timing.

feasibility.notes: Single item per prompt; minimal burden.

predictor_variables.label: Paced/diaphragmatic breathing minutes
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Slow breathing (4–6 breaths/min) increases vagal tone and rapidly downshifts arousal, reducing on-edge tension and startle.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Box breathing, 4-6 breaths/min diaphragmatic breathing for 2–10 minutes when tense.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, about how many minutes did you do slow/paced breathing (e.g., 4–6 breaths/min)?

expected_effects.rationale: Immediate autonomic downregulation reduces startle magnitude.
Reduces momentary arousal and tension.
May blunt physiological reactivity when reminders occur.
Lower arousal decreases frustration about symptoms.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Stop if lightheaded; practice seated; avoid breath-holds if uncomfortable.

feasibility.notes: Brief numeric entry; optional future passive timer integration.

predictor_variables.label: Mindfulness/grounding use
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Present-moment, nonjudgmental attention and sensory grounding reduce threat monitoring and reactivity.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: 5-senses grounding, mindful breathing, brief body scan, orientation to present cues.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you use mindfulness/grounding (e.g., 5-senses, body scan, mindful breaths)?

expected_effects.rationale: Grounding counters startle amplification via attentional reset.
Decreases subjective on-edge feelings.
Improves tolerance of reminders and dampens reactivity.
Nonjudgmental stance reduces secondary distress about symptoms.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Keep practices brief and non-invasive; avoid prolonged eyes-closed if uncomfortable in public.

feasibility.notes: Single Likert per prompt; low burden.

predictor_variables.label: Cognitive reappraisal use
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Reattributing sensations (e.g., ‘my body is sensitive, not in danger’) reduces perceived threat and distress.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Labeling sensations, probability reframe, safety reframe, normalizing startle.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you reframe or reinterpret your arousal as safe/temporary?
measurement.notes: Captures skill application without content capture.

expected_effects.rationale: Reduced perceived threat may modestly reduce startle magnitude.
Threat reinterpretation can lower on-edge feelings.
Reframing reminders as safe reduces physiological reactivity.
Directly targets secondary distress about symptoms.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Keep reappraisals realistic; avoid invalidating experiences.

feasibility.notes: Single Likert per prompt; clear anchors.

predictor_variables.label: Gentle trigger approach (graded exposure)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Planned, gentle approach to avoided cues reduces conditioned responding via inhibitory learning; may transiently raise arousal.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Small, safe steps toward benign cues (e.g., walking in mildly busy areas with supports), brief practice, plan exit options.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you gently approach situations or cues you usually avoid because of jumpiness?
measurement.notes: Nonlinear effects expected (short-term up, longer-term down).

expected_effects.rationale: May briefly increase startle during/after practice, with delayed reductions.
Tension may spike acutely, then decrease with learning.
Core target for reducing conditioned reactivity via inhibitory learning over days.
Distress may rise during practice and fall as mastery increases.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: User-led pacing; avoid dangerous contexts. If distress is overwhelming or safety is uncertain, stop and seek guidance.

feasibility.notes: Single Likert per prompt; coachable via brief instructions.

edges.notes: Next-day effect of sleep quality on startle.
Sleep quality inversely related to on-edge tension the following day.
Rested baseline lowers cue-reactivity.
Sleep buffers negative affect/distress.
Acute stimulant effects heighten startle.
Caffeine increases jitteriness/on-edge feelings.
Elevated arousal can potentiate reactivity to reminders.
Caffeine-related jitteriness may raise frustration about symptoms.
Vagal upshift reduces startle magnitude.
Rapid downregulation of arousal lowers on-edge state.
Breathing can blunt cue-reactivity when reminders occur.
Lower arousal reduces distress.
Grounding reduces amplification of startle via attention control.
Mindfulness reduces momentary tension.
Improves tolerance of reminders; dampens reactivity.
Nonjudgment reduces secondary distress.
Threat reinterpretation modestly reduces startle.
Lower perceived threat reduces on-edge feelings.
Cognitive reframing can weaken cue-reactivity.
Directly targets distress about symptoms.
Possible short-term increase, delayed decrease with learning.
Acute tension rise during approach; later reductions.
Primary target for long-term reduction in cue-reactivity.
Distress may transiently rise then fall as mastery grows.

expected_floor_ceiling_risks: C03 may have low values if reminders are infrequent; Likert design mitigates by allowing 1=not reminded.
P03 minutes may be zero-inflated on non-practice days.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use since-last-prompt framing and jitter to increase event capture.
Provide brief in-app nudge suggestions to practice breathing/grounding when tense to create natural variance.
If C03 is persistently floor, consider adding a binary reminder flag or broaden to similar safety cues.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: C06 Expectation–reality mismatch (slower-moving; captured via P05 and C05)
Physical activity minutes (initially excluded to keep burden low)
Generic social support items (less proximal to startle; add later if needed)
Passive HRV or noise sensors (phase 2 if compliance is strong)
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: 10 variables total (4 criteria, 6 predictors) with ~105 momentary samples and 21 morning diaries over 21 days is suitable for person-specific gVAR. Transformations (z-scoring, log1p for counts) and potential detrending will support model assumptions.

safety_notes: This is not a diagnosis or treatment. If symptoms sharply worsen, if you experience panic, intrusive memories you cannot manage, thoughts of self-harm, or safety concerns, seek professional help or urgent care. Keep all exposure steps small and in clearly safe contexts; stop if overwhelmed.
